title: The Pleasure Syndrome
subtitle: Understanding Desire and the Path to Contentment
epigraph: Contentment is the mind without desire.
paragraphs: Yesterday evening we went through the various techniques and reminded ourselves that there's nothing mystical about them—they're just skillful means. That's how they're referred to in the tradition: upaya, skillful means to bring ourselves into the present moment.

Like all skills, you've got to work at them. Slowly they start working for you—that's the point, isn't it? Slowing down, getting in the habit of just noting: these two especially are really quite powerful for centering attention on what you're actually experiencing in this present moment.

Tonight we can have a look at the hindrances. That's the Buddha's word for them. They're hindrances in the sense of stopping us from concentration—they drag us away from the moment because that's their job. Their job is to keep us occupied on something else.

When we're doing vipassana as such, we can see the hindrances as ways to get to know ourselves. It's often the case that when we begin to meditate, we get in touch with emotions we might not have fully accepted before. Often our family history has forbidden us to feel things. Crying, being angry, being jealous—all these things, depending on your situation, have taught you to push things away, not to really look at them. But unfortunately they remain at that subliminal level and affect our actions. They put a pressure on what we're doing.

Take jealousy, for instance. If we're jealous of somebody but we've never looked at it, if we've pushed it away and felt this subliminal guilt, we might find ourselves over-praising. We compensate for it. All these things, I think, were very well understood by Freud, actually. He captured all that very well.

One of the things about our meditation is really to open up to ourselves and receive whatever we've been suppressing. Often when you work with Eastern teachers—in fact I've just received an email about this—they tend to push effort and concentration. And this often means the meditator ends up suppressing stuff.

In this particular email, the instructions from the Burmese teacher were: if an emotion comes, you note it—anger or happiness—and then you immediately go back to the breath. The aim is to center the whole attention on the three characteristics, especially impermanence. In this way a person can be pushed towards some sort of deep insight, but it's at the expense of not purifying the heart.

In the Buddha's teaching, purification of the heart has to go hand in hand with insight. You can't leapfrog into liberation. There has to be a complete clearance of the heart. We say the path is sīla-samādhi-paññā. Sīla is your conduct, your behaviour—this manifests your attitude, your virtue or lack of it. Samādhi is the training of the mind to be still, which is just part and parcel of the vipassana process. And paññā is the insight which liberates.

Whether we like it or not, we have to go to hell before we get to heaven. It's just one of those things—there has to be a process of purification. And what we discover is that the only way to purify the heart is to let it speak, to let it manifest its feelings. That's painful for us; we don't like doing that. It's hard to be with depression, anger, the whole gamut of human suffering, which everybody has to some degree or other, I think.

When the Buddha delineated these emotional attitudinal states, he just called them the hindrances. The first one, not surprisingly, has to do with desire. Looking at the process of desire, we find that when something comes up that we like, we want it. Very simple, isn't it? You like it, so you want it.

Now why do we do that? Because when we get what we want, we're happy. It gives us a certain gratification. And what happens is that there's an underlying belief: if only you can get what you want, you'll always be happy. And you're into the great consumerist society.

Adverts play on that to the nth degree, of course. They stick desires together. You always get these beautiful men and women standing next to the car you want—the suggestion of erotic and romantic happiness which goes with a Jaguar.

These desires we have aren't just simple wanting. They're part of a root theology, a way of looking at life—our whole relationship to our lives. Happiness, for heaven's sake. What do you want if it's not happiness? Ask anybody what they want to be, and they'll say happy. They'll define it—good job, nice relationship, and so on, a big list of things. But basically, everybody wants to be happy.
And the mistake, the great mistake, is to believe that the sensual world—which is all we experience—can deliver that happiness.

Whether the world exists 'out there' is in a sense irrelevant. What matters is how we experience it. And it comes through our senses; therefore, from the inner point of view, we live in a sensual world. When the senses deliver pleasant feelings, pleasant states of mind, then we're happy.

The confusion, of course, lies in the fact that happiness is an emotional state. So we're always chasing the next emotional state, the next high. In our society that's become a disease, hasn't it? It leads to compulsive action, addiction. The whole of life becomes about what you're going to do this Saturday, where you're going to go this Sunday, where you're going for your holiday. Have you seen this film? Have you read that book? It's always: what's the next thing that's going to bring me this little high?

And the downside, of course, is when there isn't one. Because we like living on these little highs—what's the next thing to be done—and when they're not there, there's disappointment, a sense of loss, a grief. Like, what am I going to do?
Worse than that, because of the inbuilt obsolescence of these pleasures—the fact that they can't maintain that same buzz you got the first time—they lead to an easy boredom.

I read an article where a woman described her first go at crack, as she called it, 'riding the dragon.' And then she said, after that, you're always chasing that primary experience, but you never get it. That's the problem with addiction. You're always trying to get back to that primary wow experience you had.

And of course, when it comes to drugs and whatnot, the body becomes immune to them, hardened to them—it finds a way of absorbing the shock. When a person comes off drugs, they sometimes forget how resistant their body had become. And often when they slip back into the old habit for one reason or another—they could be rejoicing—they kill themselves. They overdose.

Now in a less dramatic way, that's what we're doing all the time. We're seeking happiness in sensual pleasure, and we get to a point where we're bored. So boredom says: seek, seek more pleasure. Variety is the spice of life. Off you go. And there's this incessant search.

We don't want to feel boredom. It's not a pleasant state to be in. And if it gets too much, if it gets really bad, you lose the purpose of living. It moves towards despair. It can happen on a professional level. If your work has always been very interesting and it disappears, what are you left with? You're left with this hole, aren't you? What are you going to do now? Because you can't gain that same intensity of experience, life becomes boring, empty, meaningless.

So there's a real heavy consequence from indulgence. Indulgence here, by the way, refers to all types of attachments. We're not just talking about missing your tea in the morning—I mean, that's pretty disastrous. But we're talking about heavy stuff: the loss of your job if you enjoyed your work, the loss of a relationship, the death of somebody close. All these are extraordinarily painful experiences for us.
And the pain is the measure of the attachment. The pain is the measure of the attachment.

When somebody dies, for instance, there's this thing we call grief. But it's like a huge sore in your heart, isn't it? Extraordinarily painful, a real lack. And we like to think of it as our love. This is how much I really loved the person. I miss them so much.

That's the confusion, you see—we've confused love with attachment. But grief is the measure of attachment. And if we don't see that, the grief becomes chronic, because every time it begins to lessen, you think you're losing your love for the person. You've got to re-grieve to make sure you're still really loving them.

It's only when you begin to realise that grief of loss is actually a measure of attachment that you can let it go. Then you begin to realise that love is something of a higher nature. Love allows things to be. If it was the time for the person to die, one is easy with it. You don't indulge in the grief. You see it as something unwholesome—a product of that attachment.

At a lesser level, consider objects that we own. It could be something quite costly, like a car or a laptop, or something not so costly, like your mobile. Somebody steals it. You feel angry about it—bloody thief. And there might be some grief. You go around saying, 'Do you know what? Somebody stole my phone, my mobile.'

But it isn't your mobile anymore. It belongs to the thief. They're using it. They're the ones who have the mobile. Yet there's still this figment in the imagination that it's still mine—which is a social construct, isn't it? A legal arrangement we've come to with each other: if I say this is mine, it belongs to me. And somehow there's a feeling that it belongs to me in a deep way, as if it's attached to me in a visceral, emotional sense.

But when we look at something like a clock or a mobile, actually all you can do with objects is use them. To own them is a painful thing, emotionally I mean. We might legally own it—fine, that's not a problem. But the emotional ownership, that's where the suffering lies.

So one of the first things is to begin to see that psychology within ourselves. That's why I'm so intent on this business of eating, of noticing when we drink something—because right there we can see it.

Supposing tomorrow, for instance, when it came to five o'clock, you said to yourself: 'Well, I won't have any tea. I won't even have the whiskey. I'll have a cup of cold water and I'll sit here and watch the pain.' What we're watching is the attachment.

Now the great thing about these attachments is that once you stop indulging them, they actually do die away. There's a whole process of deconditioning. And very quickly you become accustomed to a new order, once you've set your heart to it.

Here, for instance, you've come into a very strange sort of regime. You get up at 3:30, breakfast, lunch, sit here, walk. Crazy, really, when you think about it. If you described it to a person who knows nothing about meditation, they'd think you must be a bit off. But even within three or four days, you just get up, you just do it. You re-habituate to something new.

And really that's the trick when it comes to habits we find difficult—knowing that all you have to do is go through the pain of detoxing. And replace it, of course, with something more wholesome. Because unfortunately we have time; we keep moving from one moment to the next. You've got to fill it up with something. And if you can find something more skilful than what you were doing, then of course that's going to have a psychological effect. It's going to create a certain contentment.
And contentment is a big word in the spiritual world. Contentment is the mind without desire. The mind without desire.

When we say desire here, remember, we're translating one particular word: taṇhā. There's another word in Pali, chanda, which can go either way—it can be a good desire or a bad desire. If we want to sit in meditation, that's a good desire, and we should reinforce it. The desire that causes problems is something different: it's the belief that something sensual, something of this phenomenal world, is going to deliver perfect happiness.

So when we're eating, we're trying to make that distinction. We're not seeking happiness in food, but we find that when we eat, a certain appreciative joy arises. And that's natural to the process. It's natural for the body to feel good when you feed it something, and it's natural for the mind to resonate that goodness.

This understanding extends even to relationships. There's a case where the Buddha is asked: when people listen to your Dharma and don't receive it, when they walk away from it, how do you feel? He says, fine—I don't feel unhappy or anything. And the reason is his understanding of karma. The person simply isn't ready to move that way. And it's no skin off his nose, is it? The Buddha's all right. He's got his food, he's under a tree, and he's happy. His personal happiness doesn't depend on somebody believing or following his instructions.
But when somebody actually accepts the teaching and begins to train, he's filled with joy. That's sympathetic resonance.

I think some people imagine that when the Buddha was liberated, he turned into this hardened blob—no longer relating to the world, walking around as an automaton who spoke the Dharma but had no emotional life. But it's quite plain that he did. He was quite charismatic.

We talk about the four illimitables, which we can go into more detail towards the end of the week. In a sense, all beautiful emotions, all beautiful mental states, are illimitable. But these four capture a great deal of them—love, compassion, joy, and peace. So it's not as though we're trying to get rid of the joy of life.

It's very important to grasp that. Because if we make the mistake of thinking that the body is the problem—if I didn't eat, then I wouldn't get attached—that's the mistake of the self-mortifier, the great ascetic who looks like a famine victim. They don't eat. They don't go anywhere near something lovely or beautiful.

Consider the Buddha on alms round. He comes away with his food, he's with Ānanda, his attendant, and he says: let's go to this shrine, this beautiful shrine, and eat our food there. Why doesn't he just eat down on the side of the road in the dust, or find a donkey to sit beside and be a real ascetic? He doesn't. He wants to go somewhere beautiful.

Or when he goes off to meditate by himself in the forest, leaving the crowds behind, they ask him: why do you do that now that you're fully liberated? And he says, I do it for my own quiet abiding, and for the benefit of others—as a good example.

The Buddha was of the Kshatriya caste, the warrior caste, so he knew all these kings; he'd known them as children. His father was a local ruler, subordinate to the king of Kosala—Pasenadi, I think I'm right—who became a great supporter of the Buddha. And the other king, Bimbisāra, was down in Magadha, where now you get Benares, Varanasi on the Ganges. That was another kingdom. The Buddha moved around the whole area, and wherever he went, he knew these rulers.

And wherever he went, he would sometimes be offered great food. Even we monks in the East—if somebody has a birthday or a marriage or something like that, they always feed the monks very well. You get a few dishes. And of course, if you're an ascetic, you don't want to really enjoy it. So there develops this negative feeling about your life, that this isn't to be enjoyed.

So it's really important to make that distinction clear to ourselves: the pleasures of life—sensual pleasures, the pleasures of relationship, of art, even the joys that come up in meditation—are not the problem.

The problem comes from a deeper part of ourselves—this consciousness, this awareness that misunderstands pleasure and attaches to it as a way of creating internal happiness. We call that an emotional state, and we think that's it, that's me.

So all these things that come up around pleasure are really quite important. And a lot of the misbehaviour we see in young people is to do, I think anyway, with that frustration of never finding anything truly engaging. They're always being taught—even at school, when I was leaving the profession in FE, there was all this talk about making education more entertaining.

It's painful. The teacher becomes a performer. You've got to perform. And the children expect to be entertained, always to be on this lovely high. So they forget—it's completely forgotten that education, the word discipline, is about work. It's about learning your timetables. I know they don't do that anymore. And it gives children the wrong idea of what life's about. When they hit the reality of work, and for the most part it's not the sort of work they had imagined themselves doing—like fitting tyres onto cars—it's just boring. There's no acceptance of it.

So a lot of what we see in our society is to do with this consumerism. And if we look into ourselves, we'll see the same syndromes, the same psychological states arising because of that wrong understanding. This is a real opportunity to engage with that and to keep letting go of desire.

When I say letting go, you're not pushing it away. It means allowing desire to arise and pass away. You have to be very careful on that. Sometimes the noting technique is used to back things out of the mind. Like greed. Get out. Poke in the eye. So be careful, because aversion is very subtle. Not wanting is very, very subtle sometimes.

This is an opportunity to do that. And the important thing is to sometimes wait until that desire has completely and utterly passed away, and then just taste that moment that comes after—where the mind hasn't got that energy in it, hasn't got that slight turbulence which makes it always move towards what it wants.

What we're experiencing there is contentment. The distinction between gratification and contentment is very difficult. Every time we indulge a desire, there's always a point of gratification, a point of feeling, oh great. But unfortunately, all it's done is reinforce that cycle, reinforce that habit, and then we're off again—seeking, seeking, searching.

When we're working with hindrances here, this is something you can take directly into your daily life. It's about strengthening the will. The will that is called dhammachetanā is the will for spiritual practice, the will for spiritual development. It has to be exercised, it has to grow. And you do it through the process of renunciation.

Renunciation isn't self-accusation and self-mortification. It's allowing some desire to rise and pass away. You're renouncing it in order to see how attached you are to it. And you keep letting that rise and pass away until there's no pain. Then the renunciation has done its job. After that, one tries to re-engage with that thing in a more skilful way.

One exercise I always set for people—and probably you've heard it from me before—is to get yourself a nice cup of tea, or coffee if you prefer, and a biscuit, and just sit in front of your TV when your favourite programme's on. And don't turn it on. Just watch the blank screen and feel the pain.

That pain of letting go of Coronation Street and Match of the Day. Oh my God. Just sitting there with that, knowing it's on, is a wonderful exercise. If you do that every week until the pull is completely gone, then you think, what the hell was that about? What was I getting so upset about? A stupid programme.

Years and years ago I watched this programme. It was an Australian series, a storyline over three episodes, about these young women travelling in Spain and Morocco. There they got fiddled by two rather handsome men into carrying drugs. They didn't realise they were carriers. When they landed in Spain, they were caught by the police and stuck in jail. And the whole drama is about that ordeal.

Well, of course, I missed the third episode. And still, to this day, part of my mind goes, crikey, what happened? What happened to those women? This is a total fantasy. It didn't happen. It's somebody's head. But so often when I think about it, I think, oh, one day I'll find out. I must say, it doesn't cause so much pain these days.

Really work on this. After you leave the course and take this into daily life, find something—one point of the day, or one habit you know you can do without—and just sit with the desire and let it fade away.

When we do that, two things are always happening. We're letting go of something unwholesome for us, but we're also growing in that will for the Dhamma, the dhammachetanā, which is your spiritual empowerment.

It's the same when you practise meditation. When it comes to doing a morning sitting—and hopefully an evening sit too—there are always a hundred thousand excuses, a hundred thousand very good reasons why you shouldn't be doing it. There's always something more important.

It's really about catching that negativity, that desire to do something more pleasant. It's not just that you don't want to sit; it's that you want to do something more exciting. There's always something more capturing than just sitting here watching painful emotions come up. But the ability to let go of that is what creates this will.

Will in Buddhism is your power. Power means the ability to do something. When you walk there's a power, an ability to walk. We call it an ability but it's a power. When we empower ourselves through these exercises, we become stronger within ourselves. And the strength grows—the strength to let go, to let go.

Remember when the Buddha finally got this inkling that perhaps this was what would make the break, what would bring about liberation from suffering? He'd been through a bit of despairing there when he gave up the ascetic practices. He didn't know what to do. And suddenly a memory of childhood comes, and it inspires him.

On reflection he thinks, well, this is it. If this doesn't work then nothing will. And he has enough of this chetanā, enough of this willpower—willpower isn't bad in itself, it's what you put it towards. He has enough willpower to say, I'm going to sit under this tree and either I will make this insight, actually understand things and come to the end of suffering, or I'll die.
Of course, that wasn't tested because he actually did make the break. But it took him six hours. He sat there for six hours. That's what the scriptures tell us.

When we practise renunciation, it has this added quality of building up inner strength within us. We see that in people we really admire—people like Gandhi, Nelson Mandela, Mother Teresa. These people have a deep inner power which comes from that place where they've lost a lot of attachment to life. They don't care anymore about their own life. They're there for another purpose beyond themselves.

I always remember this case from that dreadful time in Lebanon—I can't remember exactly when, but I remember all that stuff about the camps and the shootings and the killings. It would seem that right between the two firing lines, which weren't that far apart as I recall, there was a school full of children, including handicapped children or children with disabilities, as we say these days. Nobody knew what to do. It was very strange—this school was stuck there and nobody had the guts to go in. They didn't know what to do. It was in the firing line.

They brought Mother Teresa to this point. What the clip showed was them explaining to her that there were all these children here and they didn't know what to do—the school was stuck in the firing lines. After the explanation, seemingly without second thought, she just walked over to the school and let all the kids out.

Nobody had thought of that. Nobody had thought, well, I'll just go over there and bring them all out. Why not? Because they were afraid of being shot. But this diminutive little woman had enormous strength from the power of her spiritual practice. If she had been shot, I don't think she would have cared.
Another way this willpower of renunciation expresses itself is through generosity. Every time we're generous, we give away something we ourselves could have enjoyed, for the benefit of somebody else.

I'm not talking about social bargaining here—where at Christmas you give a piece of cheese and they send you a piece of cheese. Or, yes, I'll come and help you decorate, with an underlying text that says, well, I'm going to do my gardening next week. I mean that generosity where you actually give for the benefit of others completely, not expecting any return.

That's what we try to practise here, isn't it? When you come to do a retreat, everything is already received. It's already paid for. What are you paying for? It's already here. So when it comes to making a donation, it's always done in the spirit of giving entirely for somebody else.

What we're doing is saying, well look, this little bit of wealth that I have—I could have enjoyed this. I could have gone to the Costa del Sol and drunk a bit of sangria and enjoyed myself. But instead I've sent it off to this charity for the benefit of somebody I don't even know. That's a letting go of desire. That's renunciation.

The same applies to time. Just helping somebody out, doing some charitable work, not for any reason apart from just helping. That time you could have spent yourself—you could have been watching a decent film, or going out with a friend. Who knows? We might have enjoyed that time, but instead we've given it. And we might not be doing something we particularly like. It might not be something we particularly want to do, but we do it for the benefit of somebody else.
In this way, the self—which is always self-centred, always looking after itself, understandably so—is slowly being undermined.

So when we get to this doctrine of not-self, what does it mean? Does it mean there's no personality, there's a breakdown, a schizophrenic, amorphous block? You end up with no self, no personality, nothing? No, not at all.

All this self that the Buddha is talking about is the relationship we have to the world, to this body. This is me. All we've got to recognise, slowly, is that it's not me—and then we have a different relationship to it.

Every time I look at my wealth, my wealth, it becomes painful. But if I look at it simply as wealth, then I can do something with it. I can do something for myself, something for others. And I can make a distinction between what is selfish and what is self-care.

Going back to eating, there's a great difference between preparing ourselves a nice meal with the attitude of self-care, and getting in there and just eating for the sake of enjoying ourselves—becoming a glutton.

That's a word you don't hear much these days. With all this obesity about, it's a no-no word, isn't it? Nobody's a glutton; they're all suffering from obesity. They're all just obese. Nobody will actually say it: you greedy pig. It's not quite done. It's not politically correct anymore to call a spade a spade. It has to be an illness. Not to say that some people don't put weight on because of metabolic reasons and whatnot. But most people—I was shocked myself. I found I had put on half a stone. It was a great shock, I can tell you. And then it got worse. I thought, oh, there's something wrong here. But I put it down to age. See? How easy it is to be obese and self-forgiving and all that.
So this evening we've looked at just one of these hindrances. We'll be at it all week, I think. I've centred on desire because it's so crucial in our day and age.

Tomorrow I'll cover the rest of them—I won't hang about so much. But as a general overview: there are states of mind that become agitated, caused by all the negative desires—hatreds, angers, irritations, all that sort of stuff. And there are things that make the mind restless in other ways—anxiety, guilt, shame.

Whenever we come across the mind being like that, remember: you always come off the thought pattern. The narrative is irrelevant. In here it's irrelevant. Out there it may be relevant. If I'm doubting whether this is the job I ought to take, out there that's relevant. Here it's not. Because here you can't do anything about it anyway. You're not supposed to be using your mobiles. You're not supposed to be on the phone. You're not supposed to be writing.

So here, the narrative is irrelevant—that's the point. By making all this mental agitation irrelevant and keeping it to the side, we can go into the actual mental state that's driving these thoughts: the attitude, the emotional state behind them. We can begin to feel it, get a hang of it, and see how it works.

The other side is when the energy begins to fall—what we call dullness and lethargy. Over the next couple of days, I hope the business of sleep imbalance will have been sorted, and you'll be very clear to yourself that any sleepiness coming in the day is in fact dullness and lethargy, not tiredness. One can be quite strict about that.

And there, of course, the attitude is simply to stay awake and be with it, to feel it. Remember, it is an energy. It doesn't feel like an energy because it's a collapse into nothingness—something's dragging us down, a dull, heavy feeling. So the attitude is basically to stay awake as best one can. That's why you open your eyes, you stand up. And if in a sitting it just becomes ridiculous and you find yourself nodding and banging your head on the floor, then go for a gentle walk.

None of this stuff do you fight. You're not trying to annihilate it. You're not trying to get rid of it. You're not trying to overcome it or beat it or be better than it. You're just trying to attend.

See them all as patients. They're all little illnesses, and they just need attending to, that's all. That's the quality of awareness, you see: attention. You can flavour the attention with kindness. You can look upon these things with compassion. And that often softens the process, by the way—it undermines any negativity towards them by seeing that these are little illnesses, little dis-eases. That's, I think, a very skilful way to look at it.
May my words be of assistance. May you be liberated sooner rather than later. Thank you.
